data.title: Tata Motors CV shares in focus after strong Q3, management commentary
data.description:
Shares of Tata Motors' commercial vehicle (CV) business will be in focus on Friday, January 30, after the company reported a healthy set of December quarter results post market hours on Thursday.

The third quarter numbers were aided by strong volume growth and sustained margin performance.

The CV segment delivered a solid Q3, with robust demand pushing volumes higher and lifting revenues. The business also maintained double-digit margins for the 10th consecutive quarter.

On a year-on-year basis, revenue rose 17% to ₹21,533 crore in Q3FY26, compared with ₹18,478 crore in the year-ago period.

EBITDA margin improved to 12.7% from 12.4%, while EBIT margin expanded sharply to 10.6% from 9.6%.

Free cash flow surged to ₹4,752 crore during the quarter, up from ₹1,479 crore a year ago, even as the impact of the new labour code stood at ₹603 crore.

Total CV volumes grew 20% YoY, driven by an 18% rise in domestic volumes and a sharp 70% jump in exports.

Domestic CV market share also improved sequentially, rising 100 basis points QoQ to 35.5%.

Looking ahead, the company expects demand to strengthen further in Q4FY26 across most commercial vehicle segments.

The outlook remains supported by the government's continued infrastructure push, with Tata Motors indicating it is well positioned to sustain momentum and drive growth.

Shares of Tata Motors ended Thursday's session 0.45% higher at ₹470.20.

data.title: Thai, Vietnam prices firm; India rates steady on weak rupee
data.description:
Key points:
  • Philippines to buy 300,000 T for Feb delivery- Vietnamese trader
  • Indian rupee at record low
  • Bangladesh allows private traders to import 200,000 T parboiled rice

By Pablo Sinha

Prices of rice exported from Thailand edged higher this week, while Vietnam rates also rose on stronger orders from key buyers; Indian prices were steady as currency depreciation offset rising paddy costs.

Thailand's 5% broken rice (RI-THBKN5-P1) was quoted at $390-$400 per tonne - its highest since December 24 - up from $380 last week.

Traders said the price increase is due to stronger domestic prices, with exporters and rice mills waiting to assess the new crop, due to come to market soon. The exchange rate also played a small role in causing price fluctuations, the trader added.

However, market demand remains subdued, with regular customers buying only small volumes, a rice trader said.

On the supply side, production is expected to be good due to favorable water conditions, traders said.

Vietnam's 5% broken rice (RI-VNBKN5-P1) was offered at $360-$367 per metric ton on Thursday, compared with $360-$365 a week ago, according to traders.

"Demand is stable and we are seeing more orders coming from key buyers such as the Philippines and Malaysia," a trader based in Ho Chi Minh City said.

"We are hearing that the Philippines will buy around 300,000 tons for February delivery," the trader added.

Preliminary shipping data showed 128,000 tons of rice is to be loaded at ports in southern Vietnam in the year to January 26, with most of the rice heading to the Philippines and Malaysia.

India's 5% broken parboiled variety (RI-INBKN5-P1) was quoted at $351-$356 per ton this week, unchanged from the last week. Indian 5% broken white rice was priced at $348 to $353 per ton.

Indian rupee fell to a record low on Thursday, increasing traders' returns from the overseas sales.

"The rupee is helping us remain competitive in an oversupplied market, but demand is still subdued," said a Kolkata-based exporter.

Domestic rice prices in Bangladesh remained elevated despite adequate stocks and imports, putting increased pressure on consumers.

Last week, the government allowed private traders to import 200,000 metric tons of parboiled rice to prevent any price shocks, alongside government-level rice procurement.

data.title: Philippines Central Bank Likely to Cut Rate in February — Market Talk
data.description: The Philippine central bank is highly likely to cut its rate in February, as 4Q GDP growth slowdown was sharper-than-expected, Citi Research economists write in a note. Domestic demand continues to be dragged by a corruption scandal, relating to the alleged misuse of public funds for state flood-management initiatives. Citi doesn't rule out an additional rate cut in April, if 1Q GDP growth continues to decelerates. However, global oil prices will also need to be closely monitored, as any surge in prices could raise Bangko Sentral ng Pilipinas' concern over imported inflation. "This may adversely impact the possibility of any further cut in April," Citi says.(amanda.lee@wsj.com)

data.title: China's CSPC Pharmaceutical inks deal with AstraZeneca for weight loss therapy
data.description:
CSPC Pharmaceutical Group said on Friday it had entered an agreement with UK drugmaker AstraZeneca for the development of innovative long-acting peptide medicines for obesity and weight-related conditions.

AstraZeneca will obtain access to some of the programmes and platforms of the Chinese biotechnology firm's pharmaceutical in exchange for an upfront payment of $1.2 billion, it added.

data.title: Trump Threatens to Ground Canadian Aircraft and Apply Tariffs to Imports — WSJ
data.description:
By Paul Vieira, Dean Seal, and Christopher Kuo

President Trump threatened to decertify Canadian-made aircraft and apply tariffs on imported jets in retaliation for what he said was the country's refusal to certify U.S.-made Gulfstream jets.

Trump said Thursday on Truth Social that the U.S. would decertify the Bombardier Global Express, "and all Aircraft made in Canada," until the Gulfstream products received full approval. He also said the U.S. could apply a 50% tariff on Canadian aircraft if the Gulfstream jets aren't approved.

A spokesperson for the White House didn't respond to a request for comment. A Canadian government representative didn't respond to a request for comment.

The Federal Aviation Administration is responsible for certifying civil aircraft in the U.S., including planes that will be used in commercial aviation. An FAA spokesperson for the agency didn't respond to a request for comment.

Representatives for Bombardier and General Dynamics, which owns Gulfstream, didn't respond to a request for comment.

Trump's post marks the latest point of conflict between Washington and Ottawa, as relations have deteriorated this month following Prime Minister Mark Carney's decision to strike a trade detente with China, America's biggest rival. Carney also recently delivered a speech that was widely interpreted as a rebuke against the Trump administration's use of economic coercion for policy purposes.

Last week, Trump threatened 100% tariffs against all Canadian imports in the event Canada struck a free-trade pact with China. Carney said Ottawa has no intention of pursuing such a deal, and that the agreement reached with Beijing focused on reducing tariffs on China-made electric vehicles and Canadian agricultural products, like canola seed.

Write to Paul Vieira at Paul.Vieira@wsj.com, Dean Seal at dean.seal@wsj.com and Christopher Kuo at chris.kuo@wsj.com

data.title: Yomiuri: Japan's Power Producer JERA Aims to Steadily Pursue Decarbonization
data.description:
By Hiroyuki Kume / Yomiuri Shimbun Staff Writer

Hisahide Okuda, president and CEO of JERA Co., Japan's largest power producer, expressed his intention to promote efforts toward decarbonization despite global headwinds in a recent interview with The Yomiuri Shimbun. "We will strengthen the LNG value chain and steadily pursue decarbonization," he said.

The following is excerpted from the interview.

The Yomiuri Shimbun: Headwinds continue to blow against decarbonization.

Hisahide Okuda: We must not misunderstand that decarbonization is being abruptly halted globally. What deserves attention is how China is moving in this area. They are establishing a system capable of producing power from solar, wind, batteries, green hydrogen and other resources at about half the cost of what the Group of Seven countries spends. Decarbonization will steadily expand.

The United States is also focusing on nuclear power in the long term. There is a movement to concentrate management resources nationally on nuclear power, such as next-generation innovative SMRs (small modular reactors), and to build out the supply chain to include nuclear fuel.

Japan risks falling behind in the shift to clean energy. To avoid hindering future industrial competitiveness, we will strengthen the LNG (liquefied natural gas) value chain and steadily pursue decarbonization.

Yomiuri: Where do you see challenges?

Okuda: Maintaining thermal power generation is a major challenge. Power generation using renewable energy is increasing, and nuclear power is also coming back online. Renewable energy has significant output fluctuations, while nuclear power cannot rapidly change its output. Thermal power currently smoothes out all these fluctuations from renewables, but as thermal plant utilization rates fluctuate, their business viability declines.

Thermal power generation is becoming increasingly difficult, and its value isn't recognized. If this continues, no one will want to generate it. Another issue is offshore wind power. Global inflation is making it extremely tough. We must consider how to sustain both thermal power and offshore wind.

Yomiuri: The government is seeking to expand support measures for developing power sources.

Okuda: For offshore wind, we must continue to closely monitor whether support commensurate with its scale will be provided. The situation is more serious for thermal power. The introduction of an emissions trading system will incur costs, yet we are not receiving returns commensurate with the risk.

----

This article is from The Yomiuri Shimbun. Neither Dow Jones Newswires, MarketWatch, Barron's nor The Wall Street Journal were involved in the creation of this content.

YDN-M0000176578-1

data.title: United Hampshire US REIT's Portfolio Likely Offers Stable, Recurring Cash Flows — Market Talk
data.description: United Hampshire US REIT's portfolio likely offers stable, recurring cash flows, Phillip Securities Research's Darren Chan says in a note. Supporting factors include high foot traffic, limited new supply and 90% tenant retention, the analyst says. The REIT maintains a healthy leverage of 38.9% with no refinancing requirements until February 2028, providing financial flexibility to pursue yield-accretive acquisitions and underpinning long-term distribution-per-unit growth, the analyst says. With 21.5% of loans on floating rates, the REIT is well-placed to benefit from more rate cuts. The brokerage initiates coverage of the REIT with a buy rating and a target price of US$0.69. Units are 0.9% higher at US$0.55. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

data.title: Trump Says He's Set to Announce Fed Pick Friday — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Matt Peterson

President Donald Trump will announce his pick to lead the Federal Reserve Friday, he said at the premiere of a documentary featuring his wife, Melania Trump. The president had said earlier in the day that the pick would come next week.

This is breaking news. Read a preview below and check back for more analysis soon.

President Donald Trump will announce his nominee for the next chair of the Federal Reserve next week, he said at a Cabinet meeting Thursday.

"We're going to be announcing the head of the Fed, who that will be, and that will be a person that will, I think, do a good job," Trump said.

Trump has delayed making a decision as he wrestles with the issue of the Fed's independence. He wants a choice who will lower interest rates by two or three percentage points from their current range of 3.5-3.75%. But the Fed chair doesn't have to listen to the president.

"Problem is they change once they get the job," Trump said at the World Economic Forum in Davos, Switzerland, last week. Trump nominated current Chair Jerome Powell during his first administration, but has been frustrated ever since that the chair won't follow the president's wishes on rates.

Trump has interviewed four candidates for the job. They are top White House economist Kevin Hassett, BlackRock executive Rick Rieder, former Fed governor Kevin Warsh, and current Fed governor Christopher Waller.

Trump has praised each man at points, leading analysts to frequently update their predictions about who might get the job. Trump has most recently praised Rieder and said he would prefer to keep Hassett in his position as director of the National Economic Council.

Investors widely expect any of those men to move quickly to implement Trump's demands for lower rates. Futures markets indicate investors are betting, on balance, that the Fed will keep interest rates steady until a new chair takes over in May.

Powell's term as chair expires May 15. He is entitled to stay in his seat on the Fed's Board of Governors through January 2028. He declined to answer questions at Wednesday's Fed press conference about whether he will do so.

Trump's nominee may face trouble in the Senate. Sen. Thom Tillis, the North Carolina Republican who chairs the committee that will oversee the vetting process for the Fed nominee, has said he won't advance a nominee until a federal criminal investigation into Powell is resolved.

Powell has called subpoenas issued to the Fed a political pretext.

Trump has said he wasn't personally involved in the probe, but hasn't said if he has asked the Department of Justice to reconsider. Wednesday night, he shared a social media post with a link to a report by CNBC that the Fed hadn't responded to the subpoenas.

Write to Matt Peterson at matt.peterson@dowjones.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Vietnam police seize tons of fake coffee products made from soybeans
data.description:
Police in Vietnam have launched a criminal investigation into a warehouse accused of producing fake coffee made from soybeans following a raid earlier this week, the Ministry of Public Security said on Thursday.

The police seized 4.1 tons of fake coffee products and 3 tons of raw materials during the raid, which took place in the Central Highlands province of Lam Dong, the ministry said in a statement.

Vietnam is the world's largest producer of Robusta coffee, which has a bitter taste and is used mainly in instant coffee. The Central Highlands is the country's key coffee growing area.

Luong Viet Kiem, the owner of the warehouse, admitted to the police that his firm mixed soybeans and flavourings with coffee beans to produce ground coffee for the local market, according to the statement.

Reuters couldn't immediately reach Kiem for comment.

The police said the warehouse raid came following a search on Tuesday of a truck carrying 1,056 bags of ground coffee weighing 528 kilograms, which had no accompanying documentation.

Further investigations are underway, they said.

"Fake coffee products are not rare, and they can be made from soybean or corn, or even both," said Nguyen Quang Tho, a coffee trader based in the neighbouring province of Dak Lak.

"Soybeans and corn are edible and a lot cheaper than real coffee beans, but who knows if it's safe for the health to drink these fake coffee products," Tho trader.

Farmers in the Central Highlands are selling coffee beans at 100,500-100,100 dong ($3.86) per kg, around three times higher than the price for soybeans.

In 2018, police in the Central Highlands arrested five people suspected of using battery chemicals to dye waste coffee beans and sell the mixture as black pepper.

Vietnam exported 1.6 million tons of coffee valued at $8.9 billion last year, up 18.3% in volume and 58.8% in value, according to the government's customs data.

($1 = 25,950 dong)
